access.anon: When accessing the authorization page, will first be guided to sign in.
access.user: Can only approve or reject third-party client authorization requests.
access.admin: Can manage OAuth clients in the admin backend.
access.agent: Accesses restricted capabilities after obtaining a token through the standard authorization flow.

rules.auth-page-clarity: The authorization page must clearly display the application name, requested permissions, per-scope selection controls, approve action, and reject action.
rules.user-consent-framing: Users see 'what this app wants and whether I allow it', not technical protocol details. During consent, users may approve a subset of the requested scopes; the submitted allow action must not grant scopes outside the original authorize request.
rules.dcr-via-provider: Dynamic client registration is handled by the standard /oauth2/register path of the Better Auth OAuth provider; the project no longer maintains a parallel hand-written DCR persistence logic.
rules.dcr-device-only-no-redirect: Dynamic client registration for a device-only client may omit redirect_uris; any provider-adapter placeholder redirect URI must not be persisted or returned as registered client metadata.
rules.dcr-rules-from-provider: Better Auth provider configuration is the sole source for DCR defaults, allowed scopes, public/confidential restrictions, and PKCE rules. Public DCR defaults to advertised public scopes when registration omits scope. Admin scopes require explicit admin-backend client creation. Legacy rest:* and mcp:* scopes remain compatibility inputs and expand only to public non-admin feature scopes.
rules.llm-platform-oauth-compatibility: Remote MCP clients use metadata discovery, DCR, authorization code + PKCE, the /api/mcp resource indicator, and protected-resource metadata. CIMD is not advertised until a concrete connector requires it. Public DCR with token_endpoint_auth_method=none intentionally returns no client_secret; clients that require one register as confidential with client_secret_basic or client_secret_post.
rules.trusted-clients-admin-backend: First-party / internal applications prefer using trusted OAuth clients created in the admin backend; such clients can skip consent and can further be incorporated into the Better Auth provider's trusted / cached client scheme when needed, rather than relying on public DCR.
rules.public-clients-pkce: Public MCP / CLI / native clients use public + PKCE mode; the admin inventory must explicitly label trust and fixed authentication type to reduce misconfiguration probability.
rules.user-delegated-grants-only: OAuth discovery metadata advertises only user-delegated grants: authorization_code, refresh_token, and the custom device_code grant. It must not advertise client_credentials unless a real service-account authority model is introduced.
rules.client-validation-from-provider: When creating clients in the admin backend, redirect URI and client metadata validation should preferentially reuse the rules of the Better Auth OAuth provider, no longer maintaining a local forked rule document.
rules.loopback-redirect-tolerance: For public native clients with loopback redirect URIs, the server should accommodate the hostname alias difference between 127.0.0.1 and localhost to avoid DCR registration and authorization requests failing solely due to these two local loopback representations; beyond this, strict matching is maintained for path, query, scheme, and non-loopback host rules.
rules.rest-resource-origin-alias: For compatibility with older CLI and Bot clients, a REST OAuth resource indicator equal to the public site origin may be normalized to the canonical REST issuer resource at /api/auth during authorization, token, device authorization, and refresh-resource validation. The server should still mint and persist tokens against the canonical /api/auth audience, not the bare origin.
rules.device-grant-custom: The device authorization flow remains custom because the existing Better Auth plugin returns session-token semantics. It is limited to enabled public clients registered with device_code, and approved resource parameters mint provider-compatible JWT access tokens for REST/MCP audiences.
rules.rest-bearer-scopes: Protected user REST endpoints accept OAuth bearer tokens only when the token has a REST audience and explicit feature action authority: feature:read or feature:write for reads, and feature:write for mutations. Browser cookie sessions continue to use normal signed-in user semantics without OAuth scopes.
rules.mcp-refresh-resource-binding: Omitted-resource refresh-token requests are treated as MCP resource refreshes only when the stored refresh grant includes at least one MCP-compatible feature scope and the canonical MCP resource approval. Resource-less or non-MCP-approved refresh grants are not auto-bound to /api/mcp.
rules.token-endpoint-observability: OAuth token handling writes production-safe Cloudflare Analytics Engine datapoints for grant type, resource-parameter presence/count, status, and duration; debug logs retain only safe error classes and redirect/resource structure. Token values, authorization codes, refresh tokens, client secrets, cookies, raw exception messages, and resource values must never be logged or exported.
rules.oauth-proxy-for-dev: Local development and preview deployments without fixed hostnames can reuse the production site's registered provider callback address through an OAuth proxy; after the preview/local instance shares the proxy encryption key with the production instance, the production site completes the provider callback and sends the encrypted user profile back to the current instance to establish a local session.
rules.canonical-discovery-paths: Path-bearing issuers and resources expose metadata at canonical path-aware locations: authorization server metadata at /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server/api/auth, OIDC discovery at /api/auth/.well-known/openid-configuration (plus its RFC 8414 path), and MCP protected-resource metadata at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/api/mcp. Root-level entries are compatibility redirects.
rules.protected-resource-scope-advertisement: Protected-resource metadata advertises canonical public non-admin feature action scopes only. Legacy MCP/REST spellings such as mcp:tools may be accepted by authorization and token validation for compatibility, but must not be listed in scopes_supported.
rules.mcp-discovery-compatibility-aliases: Because some MCP clients probe resource-relative or issuer-style well-known paths before settling on canonical metadata, /api/mcp/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server, /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server/api/mcp, /api/mcp/.well-known/openid-configuration, and /.well-known/openid-configuration/api/mcp should redirect to the issuer metadata used by the MCP protected resource.
rules.discovery-alias-routing: Compatibility aliases redirect to canonical metadata instead of returning inconsistent JSON. A shared route-target table keeps canonical responses and alias redirects aligned as paths change.
rules.discovery-cors: Discovery metadata should support cross-origin reading; at minimum return Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * for OpenID discovery, and keep the CORS behavior of authorization server metadata and protected resource metadata consistent, reducing compatibility risks for browser-type clients and debugging tools.
rules.transport-cors: The MCP transport endpoint /api/mcp should also support browser-based clients and debugging tools using Bearer tokens: answer OPTIONS preflights, allow the MCP-specific request headers, and expose MCP-Session-Id and WWW-Authenticate on cross-origin transport responses.
rules.transport-origin-validation: Per the MCP Streamable HTTP transport guidance, /api/mcp should reject requests carrying an Origin header unless that origin matches the app's trusted origin set (public/canonical origin, localhost dev aliases, and allowed preview hosts). Non-browser clients that omit Origin remain supported.

fields: Requested scopes[] checkboxes so the user can approve a subset
Consent failure message when authorization cannot continue

rules.ownership-scoped-transaction: Revocation transactionally removes every consent, access-token row, refresh-token row, and approved or pending device-code row for the signed-in user and client.
rules.active-user-grant-enforcement: User JWTs, opaque access tokens, and refresh tokens bind the exact consent generation and scopes that issued them. REST, GraphQL, MCP, userinfo, and introspection require that generation to remain active; reauthorization cannot revive old tokens and scope reduction cannot retain broader access. Admin-created skipConsent clients are the only bypass and still require an existing user. Missing evidence and database errors fail closed. Lookups stay uncached so revocation applies on the next request.
rules.refresh-revocation-race: Refresh exchange verifies the hashed token row and exact grant generation before delegation, preserves that generation on the returned JWT, opaque access token, and rotated refresh token, then verifies it again before returning success. If revocation or reauthorization wins the race, only the old lineage rows are purged and no token can continue under the newer grant.
rules.singleton-form-parameters: OAuth token and introspection form parameters are single-valued and repeated values fail with invalid_request before delegation; resource remains multi-valued.
rules.consent-mutations: The provider update-consent and delete-consent routes are intercepted and reimplemented with ownership checks, trusted-origin enforcement, token cleanup, and grant-generation rotation. Direct provider consent mutation is unavailable; the authorization page is the only consent entry point.

fields: client.name (display name with an unnamed fallback)
Single client inventory: name/id, trust/type, status, scopes summary, created time, confirmed delete
One of three fixed client patterns that derives token auth, PKCE, consent, and end-session settings
Register button and one-time post-create credentials dialog

device-authorization-grant.title: Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628)
device-authorization-grant.description: No MCP tool starts device authorization; bearer tokens minted by this flow can call REST and MCP when the approved device request is resource-bound and scoped appropriately, including feature:read/feature:write for REST.

routes.returns: { device_code, user_code, verification_uri, verification_uri_complete, expires_in, interval }
notes: The client must be an enabled public client registered with urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code; omitted scope defaults to low-risk openid/profile scopes allowed by the client, while optional repeated resource parameters are validated against provider audiences and persisted with the pending device code.
Supports CORS (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *); OPTIONS returns a 204 CORS preflight response.
.well-known/openid-configuration already includes device_authorization_endpoint and urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code in grant_types_supported.
Supports unauthenticated browser preflight for public OAuth device authorization clients.

routes.returns: { access_token, token_type, expires_in, expires_at?, refresh_token?, scope?, id_token? } | { error, error_description? }
notes: The device polls this endpoint with grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code and device_code to obtain an access token.

If resource parameters are included while polling, they must match the resources approved through the device authorization request; resource-bound device grants return JWT access tokens accepted by matching REST/MCP audiences, but protected REST routes still require matching feature:read/feature:write scopes.
A refresh token is returned only for resource-less device grants when the approved scope includes offline_access; resource-bound device grants do not issue refresh tokens.
During polling, returns authorization_pending, slow_down, expired_token, access_denied, invalid_scope, or invalid_grant error codes based on state.

fields: One segmented user-code input labelled Device Code
